what_fails: PowerShell 5.1 treats << as a redirection operator — heredoc syntax is bash-only
what_works: Write message to a temp file then: git commit -F tempfile; OR use PowerShell here-string written to file first

what_fails: PowerShell 5.1: && is not a valid statement separator — parse error
what_works: Use semicolons with conditional: A; if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { B }  OR upgrade to pwsh 7+ where && works natively

what_fails: Wraps each stderr line in an ErrorRecord object; sets $? to $false even when exit code is 0; corrupts error stream
what_works: Do not redirect stderr; PowerShell captures it separately. Check exit code via $LASTEXITCODE, not $?. Use $? only for cmdlets, not native exes

what_fails: Interactive flag hangs in non-interactive shell sessions (Cursor terminal, Claude Code, CI)
what_works: git rebase --autosquash instead of rebase -i; git add <specific-files> instead of add -i; git commit --fixup + rebase for squash workflows

command_pattern: bash <<'EOF' ... EOF inside PowerShell call to native git
what_fails: PowerShell does not parse bash heredoc syntax — the <<'EOF' is treated as a redirection, not a here-doc
what_works: Use bash -c '...' for bash heredocs on Windows WSL; OR use PowerShell here-string @'...\n'@ written to a temp file and passed to git via -F

command_pattern: cd path && command in a single Shell tool call
what_fails: PowerShell 5.1: && not valid. Even if using semicolons, cd does not persist across tool calls if the shell is stateless
what_works: Use the working_directory parameter of the Shell tool to set CWD per-call. Or chain with semicolons: cd path; command (state persists within one call)

what_fails: Scripts that use import.meta.url or __dirname to compute paths will break if run from wrong CWD — resolved path goes to caller's CWD, not script's directory
what_works: Always set working_directory to the repo root where package.json lives, OR use npm run <script> which sets CWD correctly. Verify with: node -e "console.log(process.cwd())"

command_pattern: pip install -e . in Windows with spaces in path
what_fails: Paths with spaces (e.g. C:\Users\Administrator\Documents\LianaBanyanPlatform) may cause install errors if the path is not quoted in the shell invocation
what_works: Always quote paths: pip install -e "C:\path with spaces\pkg"; use Set-Location first then pip install -e . from that directory

command_pattern: git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "message" with multi-line message in PowerShell
what_fails: Multi-line -m strings in PowerShell require special quoting; embedded newlines via `n are sometimes not passed correctly to git
what_works: Use single-line -m message for tags; for annotated tags needing multi-line, write message to file and use: git tag -a v1.0.0 -F tagmsg.txt

command_pattern: Get-Content file | ForEach-Object vs cat file in PowerShell
what_fails: cat is an alias for Get-Content in PowerShell — it works but outputs objects not raw strings; piping to grep (which doesn't exist) fails. Use Select-String instead of grep
what_works: Get-Content file | Select-String pattern  (PowerShell native grep). For raw text pass-through: Get-Content file -Raw. Avoid cat + grep idioms from bash

command_pattern: git add <file> returns error 'ignored by .gitignore' when adding new files to a repo path with aggressive blanket-ignore patterns (e.g. BISHOP_DROPZONE/*, *.py, *.md)
what_fails: Root .gitignore uses blanket patterns (BISHOP_DROPZONE/*, *.py at line ~231, *.md at line ~217) that require explicit negation exceptions (!pattern) for any new subdir or file type. git status shows file as ignored, not untracked. Working it out from scratch = 15+ min per session
what_works: Step 1: run `git check-ignore -v <path>` to see which exact rule (and line number) is firing. Step 2: add a negation exception to .gitignore in the right place: !BISHOP_DROPZONE/02_ProjectOps/ (directory) + !BISHOP_DROPZONE/02_ProjectOps/**/*.md (file types within). For new .py files in librarian-mcp (not librarian-mcp-public): !librarian-mcp/**/*.py or scoped equivalent. Commit the .gitignore change BEFORE adding the files. Verify with git check-ignore -v again — should show no output (file no longer ignored)

command_pattern: HOT-delta reporting across surfaces — always label the baseline
what_fails: Reporting HOT lift as a single range (e.g. '+62-80pp') without labeling the baseline leaves the figure ambiguous. K477 used auto-only K475 baseline (Cranewell 12%, Covenant 14.6%), producing '+68pp Cranewell / +49.4pp Covenant'. K481 cross-universe table used cold baseline (Cranewell 0%, Covenant 2%), producing '+80pp Cranewell / +62pp Covenant'. Both are mathematically clean but answer different questions. Mixing them across A&A exhibits, Substack posts, Crown letters, or pitch emails creates confusion and invites skeptic-challenge
what_works: Every HOT delta MUST carry an explicit baseline label on first use in any surface. Two standard labels: 'vs cold baseline' (cathedral vs no-cathedral; the public-facing claim) OR 'vs prior Cathedral arm / vs auto-only' (iteration improvement, methodology-context only). Preferred public form: 'Cathedral Effect: +62-80pp HOT lift vs cold baseline, 0% MISS cross-universe.' When reporting the methodology-internal '+49.4-68pp' figure, label as 'Iter-C improvement over K475 auto-only.' Never mix labels in one sentence without disambiguation
learned_in_session: B123 (Bishop post-K481 review caught self-produced +62-68pp vs +62-80pp confusion; baselines differed across surfaces)

command_pattern: k-ablation-pattern — report floor/default/ceiling, update help-text not code when curve is flat
what_fails: When a parameter ablation produces a flat curve (differences within statistical noise), changing the code default based on nominal dominance is a mistake — it incurs cost (e.g. token doubling), risks category-specific regression, and forces downstream surface updates for a marginal aggregate win. Also: changing defaults silently creates surface-inconsistency with already-published claims
what_works: Protocol for ablation sweeps on retrieval parameters (k, temperature, top-p, etc.): (1) run floor / default / ceiling at minimum (e.g. k=5/10/20). (2) Compute SE for the binary metric: at p=0.8, N=50, 95% CI is ~±11pp — treat that as the noise band. (3) If aggregate curve is within noise AND a category regresses, default stays. (4) Update the CLI --help text to document the full Pareto surface so precision users can opt in via explicit flag. (5) Do NOT change the code default; that becomes a separate proposal with its own empirical threshold. K481 exemplar: k=5→82% (2% MISS), k=10→80% (0% MISS), k=20→84% (0% MISS). Flat curve; AM category regressed at k=20; k=10 retained as default; help text updated

command_pattern: absent_ratio computed after accumulator update in detection loops
what_fails: In any detection loop where you compute 'how novel is X to my existing knowledge?', if you update the accumulator (counter/pool/set) BEFORE computing the novelty metric, the current item's features are already 'known' -- novelty signal is zero. K482 symptom: 0 mitosis events across 182 files. Root cause: _keyword_pool.update(keywords) was called before _absent_ratio_vs_signature(keywords). Every tablet's keywords were already in the pool at check-time.
what_works: Rule: always compute detection/novelty/anomaly metrics BEFORE updating the accumulator with the current item. In K482 Miner: (1) compute overlap + absent_ratio; (2) check mitosis condition; (3) THEN update _keyword_pool and _primary_sig. Verified: 11 mitosis events fired correctly after fix.
learned_in_session: K482 Phase A/B (Miner prototype, mitosis detection failure)

command_pattern: Python Unicode output to Windows PowerShell console (cp1252 encoding)
what_fails: Python scripts using non-ASCII characters in print() calls (Unicode arrows U+2192, checkmarks U+2705, math symbols like >= U+2265) fail with UnicodeEncodeError in Windows PowerShell because the default console encoding is cp1252. Error message: 'charmap' codec can't encode character. Affects any Unicode in f-strings, log lines, status symbols, or table borders.
what_works: Two options: (A) Replace all non-ASCII characters in print statements with ASCII equivalents (-> instead of ->, PASS/FAIL instead of checkmarks, >= instead of >=). (B) Set PYTHONUTF8=1 environment variable before running Python: $env:PYTHONUTF8=1; python script.py. Option B is systemic and preferred for scripts with many Unicode symbols. Option A is surgical for individual print statements. Do NOT use 2>&1 redirect with native Python (see TS-003).
learned_in_session: K482 Phase B/C (run_miner.py arrow, verify_K482.py checkmarks and >= symbols)

command_pattern: npm install with Electron+Vite+React peer dep conflicts
what_fails: npm install fails with ERESOLVE on Electron+Vite+React stacks: @vitejs/plugin-react peer dep specifies vite@^4||^5 (not ^6) even when the package is compatible with vite@6. Error: npm error ERESOLVE unable to resolve dependency tree. Also: electron-vite@5.x peer-requires vite@^8 which doesnt exist yet.
what_works: Pin to compatible versions: electron-vite@3.x (peer: vite@^4||^5||^6), @vitejs/plugin-react@5.x (peer: vite@^4||^5), vite@6.x. Then install with --legacy-peer-deps to skip the spec-lag mismatch. Verify build success after install. Safe when build passes despite the peer warning.
